title: Al Jazeera Investigation: ICE Training Teaches Agents to Evade Lawsuits, Lacks Documentation
excerpt: An Al Jazeera investigation of ICE training documents found that the curriculum emphasizes teaching agents how to articulate their actions to avoid lawsuits, that FLETC training materials describe minimal force as a 'myth,' and that ICE's lack of internal documentation of who completed training makes after-the-fact accountability nearly impossible.
content:
Journalist Lila Hassan, writing for Al Jazeera, obtained and reviewed ICE training documents from the Federal Law Enforcement Training Centers (FLETC) and analyzed them in the aftermath of the Minneapolis shootings.

  • Training materials describe 'deadly force can only be used as a last resort' as a myth, instructing that the law requires 'objectively reasonable force, not minimal force' — and that providing a warning or using less force first 'could create an unnecessary risk for the officer.'
  • At least four separate instances in ICE training lessons, quizzes, and podcast transcripts coached agents on how to articulate their actions to withstand Fourth Amendment scrutiny — framing legal knowledge as a self-protection tool rather than a civil-rights guardrail.
  • ICE's lack of documentation on which agents completed which training modules means that when a use-of-force incident occurs that contradicts training, the agency has no reliable record to identify gaps or target retraining.

Former ICE officials interviewed noted that the absence of documentation is a 'huge barrier' to systemic improvement, as the agency cannot link field behavior to training outcomes.
mediaPlaceholder: Al Jazeera investigative report with excerpts from FLETC training materials obtained by reporter
